en: Hello and welcome friends, I am Akash and you are watching Learn Exams Seekte Ho English Through Hindi.
Welcome to one more English lesson.
In today's lesson, we will learn some 10 English idioms and these idioms are very interesting
idioms. In fact, I use these idioms in my conversation everyday.
So, let's start today's lesson.
So, as I told you that today we will learn 10 English idioms which you should know.
And I have chosen these idioms a lot because they are very interesting idioms and you will
enjoy using them a lot. But before starting the lesson, a very important thing.
If you want to download the PDF of this English lesson and if you want to take a quiz on the
mobile app, I have left the link in the description. It is very simple.
You have to go to that link. You will see a course and there will be a button.
It will say free access. Click on that. Follow all the steps.
Create an account.
As soon as you enroll in that course, you will get an email and in that email, you will
get the link of our app which you can download.
You can see it on the website as well as on the mobile app.
You can download all my lessons, quizzes and PDFs.
So, let's see today's first idiom.
It's the best thing since sliced bread.
Have you heard about this? It's the best thing since sliced bread.
What does it mean?
It means something that is really good.
Something that is really good.
In the olden days, the bread used to come like a big loaf.
Right? And after that, it had to be cut.
But what used to happen while cutting? It used to become mushy.
But later, machines came.
And automatically, slices of bread used to come to our house.
So, after that invention, what I am seeing now is the best thing.
Let's see an example. Our app will be a big hit.
It's the best thing since the sliced bread.
Our app which is going to be launched or is going to be launched.
This is really a very good app.
It's the best thing since sliced bread.
It's a very interesting idiom. Now, it's your turn.
Use this idiom and tell me a sentence in the comments.
Something that you think is really good.
And you haven't seen such a thing. Let's move on to the second idiom.
And that is, take it with a pinch of salt.
Something that you take with a pinch of salt.
This is the actual translation. But what does it mean?
It means, don't take it seriously. Take it with a pinch of salt.
It means, don't take it seriously. Or don't take what he is saying seriously.
Or don't take this thing seriously. Let's see an example.
Riya's reputation is such that whatever she says, everyone takes with a pinch of salt.
I mean, no one takes Riya's words seriously.
Whatever she says, people take it with a pinch of salt.
They don't take it seriously. See, what an interesting idiom.
Make a sentence quickly and write it in the comments. And let's move on to our next idiom.
Which is, come rain or shine. This is my favorite idiom.
Come rain or shine. What does it mean?
It means, no matter what happens.
It means, no matter what happens, I will do this.
Or no matter what happens, we have to be successful.
Come rain or shine. It means, whether it rains heavily or the sun shines heavily.
We will do this. We will come out.
This is what it means. If we see the literal meaning. Let's see an example.
I will pass the entrance exam, come rain or shine.
No matter what happens, I will pass this entrance exam.
Or I will top the exams.
Come rain or shine.
The next idiom is, go down in flames.
It means, to go down in flames.
This is what it means. To fail spectacularly.
It means, to fail very badly. This is what it means.
Go down in flames. It is possible that you have worked very hard.
And even after that, you failed.
So where did all the hard work go? It went into the fire.
It went into the flames.
All our work on the project is just gone down in flames.
All the hard work we did on this project, it all went to waste.
Friends, the next idiom is, spill the beans.
Spill the beans. If you see here, there is a bowl.
And there are beans in the bowl. You can say beans.
And if I shake the bowl, what will happen?
All the beans will fall out.
This is what it means. There is a very good saying in Hindi.
Don't keep anything in your stomach. If someone tells you a secret, you will tell him.
If someone tells you not to tell, you will tell him.
Because you can't keep that secret. It means, to give away a secret.
Let's see an example.
This stays between us. I don't want you to spill the beans.
Come rain or shine. What a great sentence.
We have used two idioms in one sentence.
The first is, don't want you to spill the beans.
And the second is, come rain or shine. What does this mean?
This conversation should remain between us.
No matter what happens, you won't tell anyone.
You will not spill the beans.
Let's move on to the next idiom.
And the next idiom is, wind someone up.
Wind someone up. What does this mean?
It's a very interesting idiom.
To annoy someone by asking unnecessary questions.
We have a lot of such friends. Who annoy us by asking unnecessary questions.
This means, to wind someone up.
To annoy someone by asking unnecessary questions.
To wind someone up. Let's see an example.
He keeps complaining. And it really winds me up.
It really winds me up.
It means, he keeps arguing.
He keeps arguing about everything.
And it really annoys me.
It winds me up.
Let's move on to the next idiom. The next idiom is, once in a blue moon.
I am sure you must have heard this idiom.
It means, something that happens rarely.
It means, something that happens rarely. Once in a blue moon.
When do we see a blue moon? It's very difficult to see a blue moon once in a blue moon.
Or when do we see a full moon? Once in a blue moon.
It means, something that happens rarely.
For example, at least remember to call me once in a blue moon.
At least remember to call me once in a blue moon.
A friend of mine often says this.
At least remember to call me once in a blue moon.
The next idiom is, Through thick and thin.
Through thick and thin. This is also my favourite idiom.
If you look at this smiley.
If you look at this side, he is smiling. But if you look at that side, he is sad.
It means, to be together in good times and especially bad times.
It means, being together, being united.
In good times and bad times.
And it's such a good thing. Through thick and thin.
Here, thick means good times.
And thin means bad times. Bad times.
Let's see an example. I can trust my brother blindly.
After all, we have been through thick and thin.
I can trust my brother blindly.
Because we have seen a lot of good and bad times together.
We have seen thick and thin.
Thick means good times. Thin means bad times. It's such an interesting idiom.
Write a sentence in the comments and tell me. And let's move to the next idiom.
Sit on the fence. Sit on the fence.
If you look at this image. There is a boy.
There is a fence. Fence means, the boundary or the wall that is formed in the compound.
And he is sitting on it. What does it mean?
It means, to be indecisive about something.
Assume that he is sitting on the fence. He is thinking, should I go this way?
Or should I jump this way? Or which way should I jump?
Means, to be in the middle. Not being able to think, not being able to take a decision.
This is what it means. Right. To be indecisive.
Indecisive means not being able to take a decision. It can be due to some confusion.
Or it can be due to confusion. Let's see an example.
Sam is sitting on the fence. About getting married to Riya.
In fact, the boy is thinking. Should I marry Riya or not?
Should I or not? He is still thinking about it.
Let's move towards the next idiom. The ball is in your court.
The ball is in your court.
Now see here. It is like a volleyball stadium.
And the ball is in the middle. And if this ball goes here.
It will go towards a team. What does it mean? The ball went to their court.
But what does it mean? It means.
Now it's up to you to make the next move. If the ball went to their court.
What will the opposite team do? They will try their best to put it in the basket.
Right. They will take action towards it. Will take a move. Right.
So this means. Now taking a decision.
It depends on you. Now it's up to you to make the next move.
Let's see an example. The boss has asked him to apologize to the client.
Now the ball is in his court.
Here it will be balls. Not balls. Now the ball is in his court.
It means he might have misbehaved with the client.
And the boss said. You apologize to him.
But he is saying that he won't apologize. If he doesn't apologize, the job will be lost.
If he apologizes, everything will be fine. So the ball is in his court.
It means the decision is up to him. So friends, these were today's 10 interesting idioms.
And I am sure you will have a lot of fun using them in conversation.
Do take all these idioms. Make at least one sentence in your notebook.
Take a quiz for this lesson. Download the PDF.
And keep your English practice continued.
I will meet you soon in another English lesson. Till then as usual.
Keep learning English. Keep practicing English. And take care.
And if you have made it till here, then do like the video.
Bye.
